title: The Selfish Gene
subtitle: The Gene's-Eye View of Evolution and the Birth of Memetics
author: Richard Dawkins
themes: Gene-centered view of evolution — genes are the primary unit of selection
Altruism explained through kin selection and inclusive fitness
The selfish gene as a metaphor, not a claim about conscious motives
Evolutionarily Stable Strategy (ESS) as a framework for behavioral evolution
Memes as cultural replicators — units of imitation and cultural evolution
The extended phenotype — genes exert influence beyond the individual body
Cooperation emerging from competition at the gene level
awards: Michael Faraday Award of the Royal Society (1990)
Nierenberg Prize for Science in the Public Interest (2009)
Listed in The Guardian's 100 Greatest Nonfiction Books
Included in Nature's 'Books that changed science'
excerpt: We are survival machines — robot vehicles blindly programmed to preserve the selfish molecules known as genes. The metaphor of the selfish gene challenges us to see evolution from the perspective of DNA: immortal replicators that use bodies as temporary vehicles in their relentless drive toward posterity.
